findings_details.title: mirror.sh uses set -uo pipefail instead of set -euo pipefail
findings_details.body: mirror.sh line 5 uses 'set -uo pipefail' but this script has no aggregation/orchestration pattern — it is a simple write/append utility with two commands that should fail fast. Repo convention (CLAUDE.md): 'New scripts should choose flags by behavior: aggregation/orchestration → -uo; fail-fast scripts → -euo.' Impact: a failed cp or mv in cmd_write silently continues instead of propagating the error, masking mirror corruption. Fix: change 'set -uo pipefail' to 'set -euo pipefail' on line 5.

findings_details.title: _count_entries helper interpolates path into python3 -c string literal
findings_details.body: Line 43: _count_entries() uses python3 -c "...open('$1')..." — the path is interpolated directly into the Python string literal. Every other python3 -c invocation in this file and its siblings passes data via environment variables (e.g., AT_LOCAL="$LOCAL" python3 -c "...os.environ['AT_LOCAL']..."). A path with a single-quote in it (unlikely but possible from mktemp on some platforms) silently corrupts the call. More importantly it is inconsistent with the established repo pattern. Fix: rewrite _count_entries to pass the path via an env var: COUNT_PATH="$1" python3 -c "import json,os,sys; print(len(json.load(open(os.environ['COUNT_PATH']))['entries']))" 2>/dev/null || echo 0

findings_details.title: Path traversal guard does not handle NUL-byte-embedded subpaths
findings_details.body: The case pattern '*..* | /*)' correctly blocks '../etc/passwd' and 'foo/../../bar' (both contain '..'). However, a subpath with an embedded NUL byte — e.g. 'safe\x00/../etc/passwd' — would be silently truncated by the shell at the NUL, stripping the traversal suffix before the case guard sees it. The resulting stripped path 'safe' passes the guard and _rq_mirror_path would resolve to a seemingly-safe mirror path. In practice callers are trusted (audit-trail.sh, not user input), so exploitability is low. But the guard claims to be comprehensive and is not. Fix: add an explicit NUL check using 'printf' | 'od' or validate that the input contains only printable ASCII before the case guard.

findings_details.title: case containment check treats glob metacharacters in git root as patterns
findings_details.body: In _validate_local_path, the case statement 'case "$p" in "$root/.quality/"*)' uses double-quoted variable expansion in a case pattern. In bash, case patterns are glob-matched even when double-quoted, so if $root contains '[', '*', or '?' (e.g. /Users/O[Bri]en/repo), the containment check becomes a glob rather than a literal prefix test. git rev-parse --show-toplevel is the source of $root, which is trusted and unlikely to contain glob chars in practice, but it is technically possible. Fix: use a string prefix comparison instead — e.g. check if ${p#"$root/.quality/"} != $p using parameter expansion, or use [[ $p == "$root/.quality/"* ]] which also performs glob matching but is documented as such, or use a case fallthrough with a literal prefix strip and length check.

findings_details.title: cmd_append idempotency check is defeated by trailing whitespace or CRLF line endings
findings_details.body: cmd_append compares 'tail -1 $local_path' against 'tail -1 $mirror' for idempotency. If the source file has trailing whitespace on its last line (e.g. '{"a":1}  ') and the mirror has the canonical form ('{"a":1}'), the comparison fails and a duplicate line is appended on every invocation. Similarly, if either file has CRLF line endings, tail -1 returns a line ending in \r that will never match the other side. Both are realistic: editors and some JSON writers produce trailing whitespace; Windows-side tools produce CRLF. The JSONL audit stream then silently accumulates duplicate entries that inflate entry counts and confuse report generation. Fix: strip trailing whitespace and \r from both sides before comparison — e.g. last_src=$(tail -1 "$local_path" | tr -d '\r' | sed 's/[[:space:]]*$//') — and apply the same strip before writing.

findings_details.title: No lockfile: concurrent cmd_append from sibling worktrees can corrupt the mirror JSONL
findings_details.body: cmd_append performs a TOCTOU read-then-append: it reads tail -1 of the mirror, decides whether to append, then appends with >>. Two sibling worktrees (same issue, different branches) calling cmd_append simultaneously can both read the same last line, both decide the new line is not yet present, and both append — producing a duplicate. The plan for #604 mentioned a per-issue advisory lockfile; the implementation omits it. cmd_write uses cp+mv (atomic rename, no window) so it is unaffected. cmd_append is the only vulnerable path. Fix: acquire a per-mirror advisory lock (flock or mkdir-based, matching the pattern already used in audit-trail.sh) around the tail-check + append sequence in cmd_append.

findings_details.title: Shell interpolation of file path into python3 -c string is a code injection vector
findings_details.body: _count_entries() interpolates the $1 argument directly into a python3 -c string: python3 -c "import json,sys; print(len(json.load(open('$1'))['entries']))". If $1 contains a single quote, closing brace, or shell metacharacter (e.g. a path like /tmp/foo'bar or $(cmd)), the single-quoted Python string is broken. In these tests $1 is always a controlled path built from $MIRROR or $LOCAL so real exploitation is absent. But the pattern is unsafe by construction: it conflates the path (data) with the Python source (code). Fix: pass the path via an environment variable and reference os.environ inside the -c string, exactly as the rest of audit-trail.sh does throughout (e.g. AT_F='$1' python3 -c 'import json,os; print(len(json.load(open(os.environ["AT_F"]))["entries"]))' ).

findings_details.title: wc -l pipeline with tr -d in test violates rule-1 and produces unreliable counts
findings_details.body: Line 74: LINES=$(wc -l <"$MIRROR_AUDIT" | tr -d ' '). The 'tr -d' in a pipeline is banned by rule-1 (output truncation ban on pipelines). The same pattern repeats at line 78. Beyond the rule violation, wc -l on a file without a trailing newline undercounts by 1 — a JSONL file with N records where the last line has no trailing newline reports N-1. The append path writes 'printf "%s\n" "$last_src"' which does add a newline, so in practice the count is correct here, but the robustness assumption is fragile. Fix: replace with a python3 line-count that uses env-var path passing (consistent with the rest of the codebase), or at minimum redirect wc output to a tmp file and Read it rather than piping through tr.

findings_details.title: _with_mirror_lock comment omits timeout-concurrent-append risk
findings_details.body: The comment on lines 53-55 says 'on unlock failure (rmdir refused) the next caller blocks once and proceeds' but does not mention that when the 200ms acquire timeout fires (waited >= 20), _append_unlocked is called WITHOUT holding the lock. Two sibling callers that both exhaust the timeout will run _append_unlocked concurrently, which can produce a duplicate line. The comment should document this residual race explicitly so future readers understand the full risk surface.
